textPlain: And is that such a bad thing?  C's biggest drawback is its many surprises, I thought.  Some boredom would do it good.
I believe Odin's `^` syntax is a direct nod to Pascal. I for example had a prior experience with Delphi so it wasn't not too obscure.
> No package managerGinger Bill famously hates package managers, so if the lack of one is a deal breaker for someone they might as well look for a different language.

> In the code above, make([]proc(msg: string), len(arr)+1) generates a slice of procedure pointers with a length of len(arr)+1. Essentially, it allocates memory on the heap and returns a slice header, which includes a pointer to the allocated memory, along with the length and capacity of the slice.I guess things get exciting when that allocation fails, given that such a failure is nowhere checked for and the possibility of such failure is nowhere mentioned.
I can't decide whether the double spaced code samples in this blog post are intended or a mistake. I kind of hope it's a mistake.
That's literally the point of the article:> Odin kinda feels like a modernized somehow even more boring C but in the best way possible.
He says the end "I'm using the word boring affectionately"

That's true, but the keyboard issue is real. ` ^ ~ are dead keys on many keyboard layouts, requiring two keypresses to type.I think they should be avoided for common operations in newly designed programming languages.
Odin supports multiple returns and the `make` proc returns an error value that can be optionally checked.
